So I showed you, I did that one brochure once one year and the rest of the time what I've always used is my stuff, right? So client inquiry gets to a certain point, we take all the books and we like post it note certain sections of them that are relevant to that guy, we get some CDs, we get a big pile of testimonial letters, we put it on a big box and we send it to them. Well nobody sends them all that crap, we send bobbleheads, you know, who sends them a bobblehead of a guy on a bull, nobody sends them that, right? So and they don't throw, see nobody's going to throw that out, you know, I don't know what they're doing with them but they ain't chucking them, I mean I've had people tell me, you know, they couldn't find what to do with it so their kids got it, they got it on the shelf in their office and people ask them about it and they don't know why they got it on the shelf in the office but they just don't feel like they should throw it out. Well that's perfect, right? I've sent clocks, I've sent, now for me it's more about media than it is so like in book promotion so you know like we've got an, I got an interview coming up in a magazine and I started sending that guy stuff a year ago. Never really asked for nothing, just sent him stuff and more stuff and more stuff and more stuff and now once the interview is booked I've sent him my No BS Time Management wall clock, I've sent him two bobbleheads so he's got one for his kid, I sent him one of every book with post-adodate sections he should read, I'm dripping on him between now and the interview, here's what everybody else does, they sent him a press kit, two pockets, some stuff stuffed in each pocket, a little business card stuffed in the four corners and he gets like 20 of those a week, that's what he gets and they're all interchangeable. So if you're going to cold prospect and you're going to send people stuff you better really wow them with the stuff and better it not all be pretty because pretty much what everybody sends them is pretty, depending on the market some of it has to be pretty but it doesn't all have to be pretty and amongst the stuff the best thing you can send of course is a book or books because nothing has more assumed expert status than you wrote a book and they don't have to be, some of them are embarrassing actually but if you go back far enough and you can take a look at this stuff out of breaks if you want to, here's a, well first of all here's a copywriter guy, John Francis Tyg, I may be mispronouncing his name, I'm mispronouncing for years, he's got that quote that I really like, in the country of the blind, the one I met, man is king, and he wears an eyepatch. You understand the importance of the quote by the way to consultants and coaches so you don't have to know everything, you just have to know more than they do, right. But and this is a good book, you see I got it posted and highlighted and stuff, I mean it's a good book but really it's a promotion, it's his calling card, it's a promotional book and it's self published which he could have done a little better job of making that look more real if he had thought about it but there ain't much to it, it's I don't know, it's 80 some odd pages, the chapters are short, the pages, the chapters start all the way down on the page, you know, I mean he pretty much wrote, I think it's probably the fewest number of pages you can bind, so I mean he pretty much did as little as he possibly could do to be able to have a book instead of a brochure, right, and he's done very very very well, has a copywriter with this tool way back, this is really a crappy little thing but it made us both a lot of money, Bill Brooks and I did one together, say money, two of us do the same book and I don't want to have much money on time management and there ain't much to it, if you look at it like we covered whole pages with graphics, right less, gotta do graphics, chart, charts are good, here's a whole chapter, quotes, fill page with piece of clip art, doesn't matter, that's what I'm telling you, it's better than a book, and Brooks actually did more with this than I did but both of us made a lot of money from this little book, I used this for years, you guys probably haven't seen some of these have you, I bet there's a hundred thousand of these things out there, and all this is is old columns and newsletter material and monologues and stuff put together in a book and there's a lot of pictures and clip art and stuff so it's a Philadelphia, you know, with graphics and stuff so I mean I was in a hurry, you know what I'm saying, like always am so this is like slapped together on a Saturday but I mean it served its purpose and a handful of these going out with stuff now you can you know do better and we got more sophisticated over the years, this book by the way this is one of my NSA positioning books and here's how far this, let's see when was this done and here's how cheap I was, let's see if there's a copyright in it, and that's just like 1981 introduction by very young looking Mark Victor Hansen, we were both, we both had hair, this book is typeset on the typewriter, for those of you don't know typewriter is a thing but a roller keys, this is typeset on the typewriter, okay, the bright side ain't justified because I didn't want to take the time, I know how to do it on a typewriter, you got to type it twice and go back and take the units out and it's big pain in the ass so I didn't just, I didn't even justify the right side, I mean I typed it and we printed it, that's where we put a cover on it, okay, this thing probably made me I don't know half a million bucks, there's a direct pitch in it for a course in the back so a lot of courses and essentially there's what you would call my brochure in the middle, resources, services and so forth, and we just mailed this to every NSA member, that's what we did, better than a direct mail piece, this is a book I ghost wrote for a franchise or Don Dwyer, a carpet cleaning franchising and instant elevation of him above all other competing franchise ors because he's the author of the book on how to be successful in business, so the franchise prospects getting the service master pitch, he's getting like four or five carpet cleaning franchise pitches, the only guy, the only franchise ors that's written the book on how to be successful is Don, okay, better thing to put in the package, so you got to send them stuff that wows them, big box of stuff, if you happen to have a lot of testimonials, so Stephen asked me how many books is right, because Stephen's got like 19 books and some of them are like the Manhattan phone book, like the book on place is like the Manhattan phone book, it's like big, heavy, thousand pages, nobody's ever going to read it, nobody, oh yeah, oh it's really entertaining, you don't flatter yourself, it's a very good book, but entertaining it isn't, painful would be a better description, worth the pain by the way, but painful, and nobody's ever go read it, he hadn't even read it, and nobody's going to read all this stuff you send them, that's not the point, the point is you got more stuff than anybody else's got, so you got to be a genius, if he sends all 19 of his books, believe me the guy couldn't live long enough even if he wanted to to read all this stuff, but holy crap, look at all this stuff, Larry Dolan used to send, came out of LMI, speaker, friend of mine, he used to just get one of them big boxes that you get reams of paper in, like at OfficeMax now, you know the big box that all the reams of paper come in, fill it up with his testimonial letters, put duct tape around it, and send it out to the prospect, is anybody actually going to read all those testimonial letters, no, they don't have to, that's not the point, the point is the bulk in this case, now they're going to read a few of them, but after that it's like enough already, I get it, you know, and the other four guys sent me a trifle brochure, so if you are good at cold prospect, wow them, for the sake of lunch, I'm not at a real good stopping point, but we will just stop, and we will pick up where we left off when we come back at 1.15. You're up. Two questions, first topic is testimonials and endorsed mailings, you spend a lot of time on talking about controlling your message, what's said about you, I've heard you also speak about the fact that testimonials when written by you sound canned and contrived and it's better first person, but by the same token guiding them to get the message across you want, so how do you ask for what you want, get for what you want in testimonials and endorsed mailings while having a nice mix of original thought and sound? Personally, I am blessed in the sense that most of the testimonials I get are from people we have educated about good testimonials, yeah, so you know, I get a lot of good ones that aren't, you know, that don't need doctoring. And I have people I can get them from, but I don't think they have any idea what it sounds like. One answer is you got to show them samples, right? Some people make up little checklists of, you know, I don't want you to lie, but if any of these seven things are true, yeah. Sometimes you ought to just, if it's important enough to you and there's few enough of them, interview them by phone or in person, get it all, get it transcribed, go back and tweak it and then have them, yeah. We just went through this with our corporate client, Bill and I are doing a lot of work for Miracle Air and they have 1200 stores, they're in a hearing aid business and it actually frankly didn't occur to me that one of the problems we were going to face was no testimonials. Because first of all there's 1200 stores, so you would think, well, hell, everybody's got to have one, right? I mean, so I've got to be able to have 1200 to choose from, right? And I mean, it's an emotional purchase, so the testimonials should naturally be about grandkids and birds and, you know, well, for starters, they like, huh, what testimonial, you know, then, because they're not using any in their advertising, not zero, zip, nothing. A few of the franchise owners, including a couple who were already Glaser Kennedy and her circle members, had started moving in that direction, so they had like a testimonial book in their lobby and stuff, but like nothing system like. And then most of them had had fill out a testimonial questionnaire, which they had cleverly constructed to get nothing but blindingly dull, ordinary information. So the first thing they send me is all these filled out questionnaires, right, which are, you know, how would you rank your experience at your Miracle Air Center, satisfactory or unsatisfactory? So satisfactory, you know, and the essay part of that is Marge was very nice to me. Well, you know, I'm kind of looking for, you know, I'm a retired, you know, rock musician whose hearing went because of playing back up for the Rolling Stones and until I got your thing, I couldn't hear my grandkids, I mean, that's what I'm looking for. Well, they have no idea, right? So it took us a lot of effort to get them with samples, including I wrote fakes. I mean, I wrote them and said, here, go find one, you know, and I mean, we finally, through an enormous amount of pain and struggle have arrived at about 30 to 35, you know, I knew that they're there, you know, it's just nobody was doing the right things to get them. When you are actively involved in doing them that way, you're right, the trick is to avoid having them all sound like you wrote them. And so you got to like try and capture their voice when you take what that helps. Yeah. Is there a difference between how a the purpose and sound of a testimonial versus the structure and message of an endorsed mailing, for example? Not much. I mean, you really want, I mean, the only difference, the only thing added to the endorsed mailing is sort of the way the guy gets into it. I've written to you and sent you this letter because, you know, we're both members of the Alps and I know like me, you hum-to-hum-to, you know, so there's got to be some entry into giving the testimonial. And then you have to decide what kind of exit you want out of it on the endorsed mailing because that's everything from a lift note saying, read all of his stuff that's enclosed, sometimes actually into the PS. I've arranged with Barry to give you a free half hour consultation because you and I are buddies, but you got to read his stuff and call him right away so you can go all the way into the offer in the endorsed mailing. In some rare cases, I've done the entire pitch redone in the voice of the endorser. So there's like a 24-page sales letter now rewritten as if it came from the endorser. That's fairly rare, but there are times when you can do it. One of the Craigs does that, right? Uses the real estate agent as the front-face? Well, actually, he owns her. So that's- It's an endorser, but she's owned. Writing in other people's voices is, that's like the freelance copywriter's task. From a copywriting standpoint, those of you who've worked at learning copywriting say to learn to do it for yourself is not- the next step up of difficulty is actually doing it for other people because to do it for yourself, you only have to do it in your voice. I got to be able to write for 50 different people and not have it all sound like Dan wrote it. And so like having them on audio and being able to hear, you know, syntax, that kind of thing is helpful. I just wrote for one of the things we're using in Miracle, there's the original inventor of the product is like 70-some-odd years old and he's like John Wayne, I mean, he was in- which they weren't using him. He was in the battle of Balg and he was shot down three times and he escaped on a bicycle through France in midnight. I mean, for real, no, the guys are real, honest to God, you know, incredible war hero that they're not using for nothing, you know. And so I'm writing in his voice. Well, I went and got a bunch of like war history magazines and you know, because I want to hear how 80-year-old people say things and I didn't really have access to him. So I had to do some work to like get it, you know. Back at- Guthrie Wrenker years ago when I wrote for Tarkenton, I mean, Frank came out of the recording booth and said, I don't know who you think you're writing to, bud, but there's too many words in these sentences. I mean, I'm a short sentence guy, I don't need these complex sentence structures, you know. It's not how I talk and be, I can't handle them. I get lost, I don't know where I'm at. I mean, I wrote for Joan Rivers and so I listened to umpteen Joan Rivers comedy records and read Joan's book and you know, then you can kind of do it. The testimonies don't do them all at the same time. Don't sit down and do four or five of them all at the same time. But certainly if you're only doing a few and you have the luxury to do it, interview them, tape them, you know, massage them. And you know, basically you're doing befores and afters wherever you can. So that's okay. You had a second question? Yeah. I know you guys created the that IMA association. Here's the question. How do you feel, because you said ask questions, how do you feel so? How do you feel about creating an association or even a school that certifies salespeople at an itch with ascension to multiple levels like college 101, college 201, therefore creating all kinds of back end alumni meetings required continuing education for fees, teleclasses, frame certificate, brand and materials, all that kind of stuff. And the second to that is what do you feel the important components of something like that and what are the associated traps? Well, you answered your second question with your description in your first question. I mean, you like name them all. I mean, the most important component in all of that is they got to believe it's real. So I mean, look, I mean, the only reason the only reason Harvard works is because everybody has accepted the fact that Harvard is Harvard. You know, I mean, the fact that a bunch of us sat in a room at some point time and with William James and made the whole damn thing up in order to make some money. See, that's long since forgotten. And so if you're going to make it real, real association and issue real continuing education credits, you know, first of all, you're going to have some legal compliance issues, varying industry by industry by industry, state by state by state by state. If you're going to take a school position, you have legal compliance issues. Most people ignore all that stuff. But you know, if you start, I mean, for example, most states have some version of a private and technical school board, which if you have physical presence in the state, governs the use of such words as schools and colleges and universities and you know, all of that. Even on a distance basis, there's law in this field. Gene Kelly is our distance learning guy. He can on a break, wave your hand, Gene, so he knows who you are. Gene can tell you all the horror shows. And of course, Robert Scrobe, who's right up here, you asked me where he was, he's right here in front, wave your hand right there, is Mr. Association. So and he's formed associations for Scott and for others either here or not here. I think it is a long road requiring some patience. I think it's a very real and viable approach to this business. I think it is for some people, it's just an alternative front end and that's okay too. I think it is a very valuable asset once built. And all of the things you name, certifications, levels with prerequisites, distance and live learning, feeding into coaching, alumni, society's accreditation, university bookstore, on and on and on. I think all of those are the component parts. The thing that either makes it work or doesn't make it work is a sense that it's real. A sense that this is the thing in our business we do. The chiropractors in the room and the people that work with the chiropractic profession, there's a thing in chiropractor called the Parker Foundation. And it doesn't have the power it once had, but for almost a decade Parker was, if you were a chiropractor, what you did is you went to Parker. That's what you did. And so the conversation was you go to Parker, of course I'm going to Parker, I'll see you at Parker, I'll see you at Parker. I mean everybody went to Parker and Parker Foundation was like viewed as some thing it wasn't. I mean it was a thing that the Parker family put together to make money. That's what it was. But it had an accepted position in the industry. It has this benign foundation and premier training organization, an event of the year. In a sense the national convention of the profession. The Maddow brothers who managed to do it in dentistry to some extent. And so Parker was, I mean at its peak, 35,000 people in chiropractor, there'd be 5,000 people at Parker. And I mean you went for five days and every vendor exhibited and I mean they took over Vegas. And they got there slow and study and painstaking. But a huge asset, huge money. Because you now have, you know, vendors will give you money when they won't give us money under normal circumstances. Big companies will come play when big companies won't play with us and so forth. But so the second thing is creating the position of this is what you do. If you're in the, you know, like the, they don't get it quite done but it's sort of the position the chamber tries to take on a local basis and the position the better business bureau tries to take is oh you're a small business in Cleveland what you do is you join the Cleveland Better Business Bureau that's what you do. It's not like it's an option, it's like that's what you do, you know. And so that's the second thing that you, you know, that you try and get to. And then third is sort of optional but helpful is if the thing delivers something to them that is actually useful in the marketplace in either getting business or getting better business or making a sale, you know. So you are able to brand a, you're able to create a credibility device for them that is actually helpful in their business. Has some teeth can be taken away from them. Those three component parts. Okay. Thank you. You sir. I have a couple. Oh now you're not allowed to refer to notes man we ain't got all day here. Prior to the break you talked about cold calling, acquiring customers that method. When you do that or when you did that, did you? I didn't actually say cold call. I said get them cold but get them cold. There's a difference. Getting them cold. Did you then put them in your intake process or what happened once you made the contact, you sent them in the material, got the first meeting? How did you? Well as I said you can't, you lose the ability to do much takeaway positioning when you're doing that, going directly at them cold. Because it's so incongruent that you can't flip them on a dime. So when you're in one way, shape or form trying to get to a face to face, it's hard to simultaneously be doing takeaway. Now if you sneak up entirely, so you're going in to interview the CEO for a book on that auspices and now good conversation occurs and gee maybe we should do something together or so forth, you could take him back out now, drop him through a process. But if you're going to an appointment and now you're going in fundamentally to do a sales presentation, who's kidding who, you're going in to do a sales presentation. So there's not much of a process to it at that point, it relies on you. And you do sacrifice some control issues by doing that. That's why it's the least desirable way to get clients. See the perversity of consulting is this and it's why being talked about is so important. So here's the example that I always use. Let's say we're all out in the hall here on a break at somebody else's meeting. So I'm an attendee just like you are, he is, everybody else's. And I hear the two of you guys talking and you're complaining about this problem you got in your business. Say your conversation, let's say it's, let's make it something simple. Let's say it's like, let's say it's employee theft. And you're telling him about how you know your employees all steal and so on. And I hear the conversation and I've solved, I'm the guy. I'm the theft solution guy. And I know I could solve your problem in 10 seconds. If I turn and tap you and say, couldn't help but overhear your conversation, excuse me, Barry, but I'm the guy, I'm the theft guy. And I can solve your problem. Okay, you are mentally, if not physically backing away from me and raising every kind of resistance known to man, because I'm overt. And everybody's attitude, unfortunately, about being presented an idea by someone selling it to them is, is resistance. Same scenario. He's standing there. He taps and says, Sarc, I couldn't help it over here in the conversation. I don't know if you know the guy, but he's actually here at this conference, this Kennedy guy who's here somewhere. I don't know, he's in the blue shirt. He's the theft guy. He knows how to solve your problem. Okay, you will now come find me. Everything will go different from that point out. I can do pure takeaway. Well, I'd love to help you, but you know, I got to go, I got you got to call the office, you got I do all that shit. When I approached you, see, I can't do it. So it's your attitude is different. I'm restricted in the techniques and strategies that I can use in handling you. That's why cold overt prospecting is the worst thing in the world you can do. If you speak, even if you are overt in the speech, you can still be, you know, you can tell the story of, Hey, when I was over and consulting with XYZ company, I did this, this, this, and this, and this happened. And oh, by the way, I do occasional, so you can still now do takeaway, but you can be overt. I do occasionally accept new clients. And so, you know, it's not necessarily immediate availability and so forth. But if that interests anybody, you know, see me before I leave, and then you can move on, they're still now coming to you. Right. But when you're going to them, you lose a lot of the ability to do a lot of really good things. And you exchange them in trade for being an overt prospector. And while you may choose or need to do it for a brief period of time, you've got to get away from it as quickly as you can because it's the heart surgeon doesn't make cold calls. He doesn't telemark it. He doesn't send out cold direct mail, you know. And so at the top end of every pyramid here, nobody makes cold calls. At the bottom end, everybody makes cold calls and somewhere in between, there's less and less and less of it. So the minute you start doing cold prospecting in any way, shape, or form that is perceived as cold prospecting, you are putting yourself in the eyes of the prospect at the bottom of the pyramid. And so it's not that heart surgeons are above doing it, they just figured out it's not, you know, particularly effective for them. So you've got to get away from it just as soon as you can. I mean, now lead generation cold prospecting, even that's relatively overt, but now you can at least put them through a little process. So now you think about, like our IBAs, if they bring everybody in through the greatest free gift offer, right? So our piece is, our piece looks like this, okay, if you haven't seen it. So that's signed somebody up for the free three month trial, right? Then they get to a free chapter meeting. Now they see the guy in action at the chapter meeting. Okay, those first two steps were pretty overt. But now he gets to switch gears in the context of the chapter meeting to being, you know, we got a mastermind group, but we only got a few spaces left in it, you got to fill out an application, we're selective, he's able to switch gears, because he had this feeder device at the bottom, right? And so if you have to cold prospecting, you have the ability to take the time, you build a feeder device at the bottom so that you can be overt at the bottom and then at some place in time make the switch, right? Bad. Instead of having a quick question, I have a brief inquiry. It's in the, I can't take credit for it, but I won't divulge through that. It's in the area of damaging admissions, for example, divulging realities. Is there a timing or to get maximum benefit when you're doing a damaging admission? In other words, do you wait until it's over? For example, let's say bankruptcy, do you make that admission to your folks at the time? Not if you can help it. Not if you can help it. There's no set answer to your question. And if there are problems that have any likelihood of surfacing, then the worst thing in the world you can do is hide them. You know, the cover-up's worse than the crime, right? And so everybody gets themselves in trouble with the cover-up. But so there's no set answer to your question. It is more about, what's more useful to think about is how to use it, even more so than when to use it. And you want an opportunity to be able to put it in context. So again, because of these, Dan is God things on the left side of the ledger. The Dan has been and actually is horribly dysfunctional things on the right side of the ledger are useful. If this is not there, these things could be insurmountable. Right? So it's about context. Now if you have no alternative, again, you face whatever disaster you know you are facing. If not at the moment, certainly, you know, soon, not later. Right. But there's no pat answer to your question. Is there something like, you know, you could do an attachment, for example, you know, Robert Kiyosaki talks about, you know, how he did this or Trump talked about how he did this. Is there, do you do that or those are too big of a bridge to try to tie together? No, I don't think they are. You know, we had, I insisted on it when I worked with him and I stopped working with him in part because he refused to act to honor it. We had a situation with Rodney's brothers with James Tolson's business in Arizona of having been previously convicted and served five years in the Ohio State Penitentiary and prior to that having been sued by the Pennsylvania State Attorney General's office and prior to that. And I mean, and I insisted it all be on the order form. So, and people had to sign that particular place on the order form that they had read all of this horrid stuff and knew it before they bought this $5,000 course. And what it forced everybody to do was deal with it before they got to the point of the order form. And so we had sales reps and we had seminars and so forth. So now it had to be dealt with. And you would think it would suppress sales, but actually it, if anything, it might have worked the other way. I mean, it was presented well, but still. It was, I mean, it's not as ugly as I just said it, it's actually worse. So, you know, look, you can build a bridge from anything to anything, you know, you just have to figure out how to do it. It is by profound conviction there is no mess, no scandal, no disaster that is big enough to stop people from giving somebody money. It's just a question now of how they handle that presentation. Okay, thanks guys. This is sort of an answer to the co-prospecting thing too. I'm still on the how to get a client and how to get a client cold if you must, but also how to create them coming to you. The, not that anybody remembers the sales letter, but one of the things I promised you while you were here was my number one career stress strategy. And it also refers to something I told you yesterday, which was the worst news you could hear the entire time you were here, which is you got to be a good writer. The single best tool you will ever have for a whole variety of purposes, including this one, getting people to come to you and ask you to help them or advise them is a newsletter. And it allows you to put it in front of people in a herd and people not in a herd. The nice thing about newsletters amongst many other benefits is that busy successful people subscribe to a bunch of them. And in many cases, they don't remember what the hell they subscribed to and what they didn't subscribe to. So you can take a target list and start sending them a newsletter marked here is your copy of your paid newsletter this month. And even though they didn't subscribe to it since they're getting it and it says they did, they figure they must have. And they'll now pay just as much attention to it as if they had. Do you need me to say that again? Okay. Okay. Busy successful people subscribe to a lot of newsletters. In most cases, they don't remember what the hell they subscribed to half of the time. But if it arrives and says, here's this month's copy of your paid subscription newsletter, since it says that I must have paid for it. And since I paid for it, I'm not going to throw it out. See, it's not junk mail anymore because I paid for it. So now I'll read it. And so you can create a farm, a subscriber base that you deliver a newsletter to who never opted into it, never subscribed to it in the first place, but now we'll pay just as much attention to it as if they had. Now it's better if they subscribe to it. So let me give you a couple of examples though. There's somebody doing it to me right now, by the way, and it took me three months before I noticed, which is really very cool. I get a lot of health newsletters. I've subscribed to a lot of health newsletters. One, because I do copywriting work in that category, and I want to get all the junk mail. So the way to get all the junk mail is to subscribe to a bunch of stuff because then you get all the junk mail. And so I really want the junk mail as much as I want the damn newsletters, but you got to buy the newsletters in order to get the junk mail. If they let me subscribe to the junk mail, I'd just subscribe to the junk mail, but they won't. So I get a lot of them, I don't know, 20, 30, 40 a month. So I don't remember what the hell I've subscribed to and what I haven't subscribed to. I don't know. But I pay attention to them all. I also pay attention to them for personal reasons. So I notice now this month, so here's the envelope, and it littered its Dr. So-and-So's newsletter, and it's here's this month's issue of your paid subscription newsletter. And I've been reading it for three months. And I started to think again as I was reading it this month, you know, I sure as hell don't remember subscribed into this thing. You know, well, we do have a file, you know, somewhere of, so I now go dig. Well, I never did subscribe to this thing. He's retina, he's doing this, he's retina list, and he sent in and out as if it wasn't paid subscription newsletter. Well, it's the best teaser copy you could ever put on an envelope other than photo and clothes do not bend. I mean, because it immediately moves it over from the B pile to the A pile because you paid for it. It's a little deceptive, but it is brilliant. So I come home from NSA in 19 whatever with Buster Crab on the plane and I got a client and then I decided to start a newsletter. And so my very first newsletter was for speakers and it was called Marketing Your Services and it was $179 a year. And the NSA market at that time was maybe, I don't know, 3,000 people in it. So we mailed the, I mailed the crap out of them to get people to subscribe about $179 a year and I got four or 500 subscribers in a period of five or six months. And then I just sent it to the rest of them as if they had paid to subscribe to it. And I treated them all as paid subscribers. So I took the money from the ones I could get money from and I sent it to the rest of them as if they had given me money. Because really I wanted to be in front of them and build the reputation in that small community and in that small constituency. So now let's say for the sake of conversation that you want to market to CEOs of the 500 biggest companies in Minneapolis. Well, so you go home and you start, you know, the, you know, the success secrets newsletters for CEOs of large Minnesota companies. And you pick your hit list and you make sure you got their names and titles and all that stuff right, even if you got to do phone calls in order to get it, because if they look perfect, it's got to be perfect. And then you start sending them their paid newsletter every month that they never subscribed to. And before you know it, you're going to be getting responses for things you lead, generate for in the newsletter. And pretty soon somebody's going to call you up and say, can you come over to my company and talk about this? And they will never know that they weren't a paid subscriber and you need never tell them. If I need to, I could do that a third time for you, but you shouldn't have to hear it a third time. And so I have never been without at least one newsletter since 1978. And in some cases, I have had three or four or five, some have been real, some have not been real, meaning some have people been paying for them. Some people have only thought they have paid for them. And some have been constructed for very small constituencies for a brief period of times, only to set up the entry into those constituencies. I briefly flirted about a year, a year and a half ago with doing something with all the freelance professional copywriters there are in the country. There's a bunch of them in one directory, so they're like easy to find, which is what makes it kind of, which is what tempted me is it's like, it's almost like deja vu of NSA for me. I mean, I'm one of them. I know what they, I know how they think, I know how they behave. Here they are all in one place. So I don't have to go hunt for them. I know most of them are starving, which is good news. They actually publish their fees. So I know who's getting paid what, they publish their clients. I know who they're working for. For me to go after them would be formulaic. One of the things I would do if I was going to go after them is I would start a newsletter for professional copywriters. I would take a run through that list and get as many of them to subscribe as possible for money. And then I would treat them all as if they had subscribed for a period of time. And then I would set them up for the series of kills. Be that seminar, be that coaching program, whatever. I talk myself out of it because I don't have enough time to do it. But if I was going to do it, that's what I would do. You should see the list of stuff I talk myself out of. It's not as long as it needs to be. But you want to see the list of the things I talk myself out of. Over the years I've had marketing services for speakers. I've had a practice building secrets newsletter for chiropractors, dentists, veterinarians, and pediatrists when we went into the chiropractic and dental fields. And that's had as many by the way as 2,000 and some I've paid subscribers. And the before runner to the No BS letter was a thing called the business secrets letter. And then the No BS marketing letter has been around since 1991. Much more primitive than what you see today, but nevertheless. And of course now the No BS marketing, the affluent letter, the info letter, these have produced well over either directly or indirectly, these have produced well over half of all of my money. They are the foundation for everything. And again, IBAs in the room have an advantage in their local consulting businesses and their coaching businesses in a sense because we're providing that for them. So they already got a foundation. But as you grow and get bigger and more sophisticated or those of you that are building natural national constituencies, you've got to do this. And one of the answers to your dilemma, wherever he went, where Mr. Cole called prospecting guy go. There you are. Okay, I thought maybe you got mad and left. This is one of the answers to a dilemma like that is again, if you must go cold, then turn cold into warm sneakily. So take your constituency, create a newsletter for them, get those who will subscribe to subscribe, then treat the rest of them as if they had. And build that relationship over four or five or six or seven or eight months. It usually won't take that long for somebody to start coming to you. It doesn't take much demonstration anymore of competence to start attracting money because there is so little of it. Forget expertise, competence. People take notice that you actually were able to get a newsletter out every month for three months. Somewhere, one of your prospective clients says, Cripe, I got the same gray envelope three months in a row. I better talk to this guy because I'd like to know how the hell he's getting anybody to get anything out three months in a row. I mean, I'm serious. It doesn't take much of a show of competence anymore in order to attract a lot of money. Woody Allen's right. 50% of it's showing up. Now, the other 50% is pretty like critical, but when you think of it, the whole first half is like showing up. Holy Cripe, you managed to show up. I mean, in the restaurant industry, you get promoted from Fry Cook to regional manager in about six months if you can manage to just show up sober. Oh my God. You're like VP in a hurry because they're like, oh, they're wildly impressed. So that really is the good news for all of us is it takes less and less and less to impress people because everything around them is like worse and worse and worse and worse and worse. But this is a leapfrog to the top of the heap tool. Because being the writer, editor of a prestigious specialized newsletter is like being the author of the book. But you don't have to wait to get the book printed. I mean, you can like do this tomorrow, you know? Now, the newsletter doesn't have to be like spectacular, but it can't be crap either. And there's a whole host of things you want to get good at doing when you do one of these things. And that's a whole other topic. But this is like this has been my key. And so if you want my key, that's the key newsletter or newsletters used in a very strategic way. Can you just wait like five minutes? You stay right there? Because I'm almost done with how to get them cold. The other way to get them cold is going all the way back to the sort of classic JP DK information business model of you lead generate with paid advertising. There's no secret to what you are doing. And you get people raise their hands and request information. This is if you've been paying attention, this is becoming an increasingly common ad that used to by the way only be in magazines for very rich people. This is fundamentally a consultant coach to help actually most of the clients are, of course, men to help men with a lot of money and zero social skills find trophy wives. That's what it is. And their consulting packages are in the 15 to $75,000 range. How they get paid is a fee plus a performance bonus on any relationship that last six months or longer. And another performance bonus if they actually get married. But it's a it's no different than so what I want to show you is it's absolutely no different than now here's a business ad. So here's I don't have a slide for it. Well, here's a classic business ad. Here's the Chauncey Hutter ad, right, which is in the tax planning how an amazingly simple accidental secret allows you to instantly triple your tax business and 30 days take a vacation during the middle of the tax season and drive your competition absolutely crazy by living your no stress making big money lifestyle. And ultimately it's an offer for a free report, right? That's all this is same deal. There's a story here's an offer and here's what to do next to get more information. Okay, it's classic lead generation advertising. And it's not it's overt. But now unlike the direct to appointment, it does allow this advertiser to move backwards from being overt to being takeawayish by virtue of the materials that get delivered when they raise their hand and the next step that gets put in front of them. So now the next step instead of a sales letter that sells a box, the next step can be an application that has to be filled out in order to get a phone appointment. You could start here, actually go backwards. But under the, under utopian circumstances, somebody does all three, right? But because if you have this, you have no pipeline. What this does is give you a pipeline where customers have a place to mature, right? Whereas when you bring them directly in at the middle or the high end, you're always bringing them in directly at the middle or the high end. So if you have the pipeline full of maturing customers who over a year or two years or three years or four years or five years are getting ready, at some point in time, you no longer have to do anything in order to fill the top two. And we ran all that at the same time we were running a seminar promotions. Thank you. The other thing, since you bring it up indirectly is what I discovered a number of times is it's a little beat the control track, by the way, in the newsletter business, as everybody probably knows, the most traditional offer is the giant bribe offer is the subscribe to the newsletter for X dollars and you get this big mountainous pile of 56 free reports or 22 free CDs or whatever. The two usual variations on that are one year, two year, three year subscription and you get bigger and bigger piles of stuff depending on whether you do the one year, the two year, the three year or here's choice of 52 free reports pick any eight free with your subscription. So those are like the most traditional things there are. In a number of cases that's been a control. I've been able to just flip the control, make the bribe package the 149 dollar offer and you get one year of the newsletter free. One works the same as the other. The client runs one through the list in January, the other one through the list in February, the other one in March, the other one in April, etc, etc. Yeah, it's a it's a good beat beat beat beat beat the control track. Well, because one of those testimonials is from Joan Rivers and the other one is from at the time a hugely successful infomercial, Perfect Smile. And I didn't have testimonials from either one of them about me. I only had testimonials from them about the book. And they are more important than what the testimonial was about. The Joan Rivers thing always caused, you know, Joan, tell me about Joan. Well, I don't care what they want to hear about as long as they want to hear something, because now we're in dialogue. And the Perfect Smile thing, because I got a lot of business out of the infomercial industry, that was extremely useful. Now, the other thing to think about is those testimonials exist because I had a book and went to get cover blurbs for the book. The Perfect Smile one is actually a secondary favor arranged by Greg Ranker or Cathy Ranker, as a matter of fact. But without the book, I couldn't have got them. One, because it's a testimonial, you know, with a story. This one's in there again, for the same reason as Hewlett Packard, it's B2B. And I have less business to business than I have anything else. So that's about as business to business as you can get. How many of you have ever been signed a testimonial with a letter of a baby in a bikini standing on two elephants? That's probably nobody but me, right? Do you think? This is in there. Why is this in there? Oh, this is in there because this is, this is now another one of these personal ones. This is a booze problem related one. And so it's actually in there, has disclosure and encouragement to connect on that level. This is here because it's Australia. And having clients from distant lands, you want to remember that, having clients from distant lands is still extremely exciting and interesting and impressive to people. You would think you still had to go over there on the Queen Mary and take three months getting there the way people react. You can actually still get a gatekeeper to put somebody on the phone by adding the words, calling long distance. Now you would think that would have gone away, but that still works. You want to remember that, by the way, if you're trying to get somebody on the phone. Infinitely better to say this is Dan Kennedy calling for Dr. Altadonna long distance than this is Dan Kennedy calling for Dr. Altadonna. For some reason that still works. It creates some kind of little sense of urgency to go get them. Having a client from a distant place is still interesting to people. The Bridget Campbell one is in there for the same reason that the Brenda Dillon one is in there. I always liked it. This is in there because it's a weird occupation. And it's an interesting occupation. So there's a testimonial from magicians. This one is in there. Not so much because I was prospecting for any work from nonprofits because I never did. Not because I wanted work from nonprofits, but it got me a football player. And then it allowed me to get pictures in of the football player and the football player with a celebrity. Well, that's the with strategy. I'm not with Ditka, but this guy's a client of mine and he's with Ditka. So that's the same as being with Ditka. Hartoons is in there because it's a really, really, really good testimonial letter. You cannot overuse these things. It's impossible. You got to get them all the time, get as many as you can, keep getting them updated as your relationships with people continue over a period of time. Use them, use them, use them, use them, use them everywhere because what they say about you is infinitely more persuasive than what you say about you. Last thing before we break. Let's have a quick discussion of ethics. Here's what I could, here's, here's my advice to you about all of this. Number one, don't take clients you shouldn't. So you ought to define for yourself what that means. But the best advice I can give you is figure out what that means for you and then do everything you possibly can to resist the temptation to take one you know you shouldn't. I don't care how broke you are. If you take one you shouldn't, all that does for you is make you broker. You may temporarily keep a car from being repossessed, but trust me, it's not worth it. The car is replaceable. Your time, piece of mind, sanity of dealing with a client you shouldn't have taken, you can never get back. I have one from 25 years ago, I still occasionally wake up in the middle of the night thinking about her and not in a good way. The money has long since been spent, the problem the money solved has long since been gone, somehow it would have been solved without her. The memory haunts me for life. The best reason not to take a client is you know you won't be able to satisfy him. Big mistake to take him if you know you won't be able to satisfy him. It doesn't make him bad. So the two examples I got here, they both for somebody would be and were great clients and I like them personally and I have enormous respect for him. Bill Phillips when he was at EAS, I would love to have worked with Bill Phillips, but I would never have been able to satisfy Bill Phillips because if Bill wakes up at two o'clock in the morning with an idea, he wants everybody on a conference call at 2.30, all the copy written and there better be a half a million pieces and full color dropping in the mail by noon the next day. Now that don't make Bill a bad guy, that don't even make him wrong, but it makes him really wrong for me. Ken Roberts people, they are not going to be happy unless they can pick up the phone and talk to their copywriter. There is no way to make them happy unless they can do that. It doesn't matter, I could make them 50 million dollars, they wouldn't be happy and I, not even for them and not even for all the money either one of them might have paid me, am I good to be picking up the phone and talking to people at random. It's easier to go find somebody else to give me money. So I know I'm not going to satisfy him. To take him and have him unsatisfied, life's too short and reputation's too precious. So figure out what for you is not a good client and then don't take him. Don't over promise and under deliver. The temptation is enormous to tell the client what they want to hear about timelines, deadlines, results in order to get the money especially when you need it. You will regret it. Don't prescribe based on ability to pay. An awful lot of consultants do this. The temptation is understandable and in eight out of ten, seven out of ten cases you probably get away with it. But eventually you go be caught and it's good to not be pretty. So I have a formula for calculating a project. It's a real formula and it doesn't matter to me whether it's a Fortune 500 company or it's a little guy just starting this company. Fees the same. Deals the same. And if anybody ever compares notes, they're going to find the only variable is the anticipated aggravation surcharge and I disclose it. So if I add it, I say to the person face to face, there's $5,000 added to this contract because I think you're going to be a pain in the ass. If by some chance you're not a pain in the ass, I may give it back to you when we're all done. So if anybody ever compares notes, it's right there. Don't cut deals. You will be tempted to do it. I'm going to let one person in my coaching group for free because it's so good to have them in there. I'm going to let them in at half price because nobody keeps a secret. Ever. Somebody will compare notes. Don't cut deals. Plus, you don't just lose the money. You lose the power. You lose the control. They either pay or they don't pay. We talked about this earlier. Be wary of recommending others. Be very careful. Be very cautious. Be very demanding. Somebody asked about our criteria. I didn't really want to have a big discussion about it. One is, of course, that people guarantee what they sell to you. We don't let anybody sell to you without a guarantee. That's really a small criteria, but it's still an important criteria. Don't steal from your clients. If you're going to use work product in other ways, disclose it. If they have never signed a non-disclosure agreement. Never. Refuse under all circumstances. But on the other hand, if they have truly proprietary information, which a lot of people think they have but they don't, but occasionally somebody actually does, it's always a great shock to me, but occasionally somebody actually has something unique or that they really are getting to the market with first. Don't disclose it. In your group settings, have clear rules and frequent reminder discussions of what is fair play with SWD. What's fair play about copying, using, and the best rule connected to all other rules if in doubt, ask the other person. I have a client right now. I have one client who paid me $75,000 to write his campaign. He's letting another guy in the coaching group knock it off. Guy asked him. He's letting him knock it off. Paid me $75,000 for it. That's fine. They made an arrangement. He didn't ask the guy for any money. If he had asked him for money, the guy would have paid him. He just chose not to. That's fine. But if the same guy had taken it all and used it and simply taken out chiropractic and put in martial arts and walked off without asking him, the two of them are in the same coaching group, he'd be madder in a wet hand. You try and get yourself and everybody else to play as fair as you can. Number seven is the best tip of all. The word avoid is the important word. Try and avoid all misunderstandings. So number one, disclose other relationships. Don't let them think for a minute. They got some kind of exclusive. They don't. Never work without detailed written agreements. Never, never, never, never, never, never, never, never, never, never, never, never, never, never. The grand's a good example for me. I've known Ron for 25 years like Ron. Ron's as close to a friend as I can allow a client to be. However, I wouldn't do work for Ron without a written contract on a bet. Not because of distrust, but because we're both old feeble. His memory's no good. Mine's not either. There go be a day sometime when one of us ain't go remember it exactly the same way the other one remembered it. Has to happen. Best way to avoid it, paper. Paper trail everything. I'm amazed at people who don't do it. I nag people to do it all the time. I nag myself to do it all the time. See, in a business relationship, there is no casual phone conversation. The very definition of a business relationship precludes casual conversation. So there is no casual conversation. There's business conversation. That's what a business relationship is. So in the hallway, on the phone, if it's anything that has a tail to it, it ought to have a paper trail to it. Tail meaning it controls something that's supposed to happen, that he's supposed to do, that you're supposed to do, that you agreed to do, that he agreed to do, or it's ever likely to come up again. If it's got a tail, it ought to have a paper trail. Now, that doesn't mean it's got to be a signed off on agreement. It just means that somebody's got a memo somebody. And since you can't rely on them to memo you, you've got to memo them. Bill just confirming, we talked on Tuesday, and you said you were going to bear the cost of getting XYZ transcribed, and I was going to take care of getting the photographs done. Not date, sign, return here, if this is what we agreed to, that's not necessary, but there's a written record, and it's in a file. So now, six months from now, somebody doesn't remember it exactly the way the other one remembered it. Well, wait a minute, I got to memo. I memoed you on such and such a day. If there's a tail, there ought to be a paper trail. See, it's poetic. It should help you remember it. Number eight, don't steal clients from others. Bad idea. A very bad idea. I have, from time to time, I have a, there's a very famous copywriter who's the best copywriter on the planet, but quite often the caveat is he never gets any copy written. And so I have, on a fairly regularly occurring basis, unhappy clients of this particular copywriter coming to me, looking for a new home. This guy's a friend of mine, and he's been very good to me over the years. I don't want his clients. I never want a situation of him being irritated thinking I stole a client. And so in 10 years, I have taken one. And I called him first. And I said, you know, the guy's here, he's pissed at you. I don't think you're ever going to get another dollar from him. You don't want to be stepping in front of his car. You know, I like to work with him. Do you want money? Do you want, you know, but I wouldn't do it without the phone call. Jeff Paul, Jeff Paul and our friends. Ben Altadonna has migrated to me from Jeff. Ed O'Keefe has migrated to me from Jeff. In every instance, I called Jeff first. This guy wants to come over here. Ben called the very first time, said he wanted a consulting day. I immediately called Jeff and I said, now here's what's going to happen. If I have a consulting day with Ben, Ben's going to want me to do some stuff for him. Some of that stuff I'm going to want to do. Me doing some of that stuff may get in the way of you doing some stuff with him. If you don't want me to take him, tell me now. And I won't even do the day with him. Once I do the day with him, you can't ever be upset about anything else that happens with him because you should know I'm probably going to say yes to anything he wants me to do. Do you want me to take the day or do you not want me to take the day? Then I paper trail it. Just a reminder on such and such a day, I called you. We talked about Ben. You said have Adam. Dan. Because I don't want a year from now, two years from now, three years from now. I don't want any discrepancy. You don't want it either. Ninth thing, not on the overheads. And then we will take our two o'clock break. The most I didn't even get it. The big ethical issue for consultants, coaches, business opportunity marketers, etc. And the thing that gets in people's way more than anything else about making maximum money in any of these fields is how they feel about the people who they take money from who don't get any results. You got to get or you will become emotionally ill in this business. You got to get that that is not your responsibility. You did not give birth to them. They will not visit you in the old folks home. It is not your responsibility to make them successful in any way, shape, or form. And it is generally a bad idea to take that responsibility on either actually or emotionally. It is a practical matter, by the way. You can't make them successful. Even if you wanted to, and even if you tried. Every population resorts itself into 515-80. You can go sleep with all of them on their couch, get up every morning, drag them out of bed, get them out. Don't make any difference. I did that when I was 16, 17, 18, and 19 in the Amway business. I am telling you, 515-80, don't make any difference. You can go over there, you can sit with them, you can make the phone calls with them, you can do the meeting for them, you can recruit to 515-80. Don't make any difference. Even if you wanted to go sleep on a couch with them, drag them out of bed, make them do it, don't make any difference. It is a practical matter. You can't make them successful anyway. Now you have to decide what is the ethical line in all of this. If you decide the ethical line is that a majority of people have to achieve some level of whatever you define as success in the use of your advice or stuff, you will still be emotionally sick because the majority won't. 515-80, the best you're going to get is 20. That's the best you're going to do. If you define it as majority, you will be emotionally ill. What is unethical is if 100% is if 20% of them follow your instructions and do what it is that you tell them to do. And do as you ask. And none of them are able to be successful. If the thing or your advice is so inherently flawed that no one can do it. If the representation of what can be done is so far from reality that no one can do it, then that I would judge is unethical. Now the legal standard for all of this, by the way, is not a topic of conversation for this seminar, but it's very different from what I just described to you. But fortunately it is rarely enforced. So you got to decide what your ethical standard is about all of this. I strongly tell you it can't be about a majority and it can't be about everybody. It is about a minority, but it shouldn't be about zero. The thing should actually work. Now there's advertising license. And even the Federal Trade Commission recognizes that without advertising license nobody could sell anything. So easy quick weight loss is easy quick weight loss only if you exercise four hours a day on the treadmill. So there's some room for advertising license in the promise, but at some point you've stretched the privilege of advertising license to outright fraud. Only you can decide where that stretch starts to occur, but you probably don't want to do it. So the thing should work for the person who really works it as you tell them to work it. Understand all the way back to yesterday's conversation there's wiggle room in that though because remember it's about their value, not our value. So Guy increases his business by five percent that's not enough to make you happy, but if that's enough to make him happy it works for him. All right let's take our our two o'clock break and let's start back at 15 minutes past the hour. We got time for a few questions when we start back if you want to be at the table up at the beginning and we are going to talk next about how to get paid.
